steps.title: Identify the defense switch
steps.content: The conclusion says whitefly-infested plants emit airborne chemicals that make nearby tomato plants favor the microbial defense, salicylic acid, even when those nearby plants are later attacked by whiteflies. Herbivory would normally call for the jasmonic-acid defense instead.

steps.title: Predict the data pattern
steps.content: Supporting data should show two comparisons against the control. Plants exposed to air from whitefly-free plants should produce more jasmonic acid after infestation, showing the normal herbivory response. Plants exposed to air from whitefly-infested plants should produce less jasmonic acid and more salicylic acid than controls.

steps.title: Reject weaker descriptions
steps.content: Choice B compares jasmonic acid with salicylic acid inside each group rather than showing a control-based shift. Choice C overstates the result by claiming exclusive salicylic-acid production. Choice D includes the lower jasmonic acid result but omits the increased salicylic acid in the whitefly-infested-air group, so it does not show the microbial-defense switch. The answer is A.
